2: The unnamed man of God from Judah pronounces a sign against Jeroboam’s altar at Bethel by naming the agent of its future destruction: hinnēh ḇēn nôlāḏ lᵉḇêt dāwîḏ yōšiyyāhû šᵉmô, ‘behold, a son shall be born to the house of David, Josiah by name, and he shall sacrifice on you the priests of the high places who make offerings on you.’ The prophecy is fulfilled in precise verbal echo three centuries later in 2 Kings 23:15-16: Josiah defiles the altar at Bethel, burns bones on it, and ‘this was the word of YHWH that the man of God had proclaimed.’ The specificity of the named prophecy — naming the individual who will fulfill it, 300 years before his birth — is the OT’s sharpest example of predictive precision in a non-messianic context, and establishes the pattern of fulfilled-by-name prophecy. Acts 4:28 applies the same pattern to the crucifixion: the rulers and crowds acted ‘to do whatever your hand and your plan had predestined to take place.’ The named-agent prophecy of 1 Kings 13:2 is the OT’s structural precedent for understanding how historical agents fulfill divinely predetermined events — a framework Peter and Paul apply directly to the passion narrative as its fulfillment.
1: A shadow: a man of God came from Judah to Bethel — an unnamed prophetic figure sent from the south to confront the northern apostasy. The pattern of the prophetic envoy who comes from outside the corrupt institution to pronounce judgment prefigures Christ's entry into Jerusalem, coming from outside the city's power structures to confront the temple establishment. The man of God came 'by the word of the LORD' — bidbar YHWH — the word itself sends him.
3: A shadow: the man of God gave a sign on the same day — 'the altar shall be torn apart and the ashes that are on it shall be poured out.' The miraculous sign authenticating the prophetic word is the criterion of true prophecy (Deuteronomy 18:22). The sign accomplished publicly in front of Jeroboam demonstrates that the word spoken about Josiah is reliable. The NT's criterion for prophetic authentication: the resurrection, the sign of Jonah (Matthew 12:39-40), the sign that confirms Christ's prophetic word beyond all others.
4: A shadow: Jeroboam stretched out his hand from the altar and said, 'Seize him!' and his hand withered. The hand stretched out against the divine messenger is struck — the principle of Isaiah 8:9-10: 'Take counsel together, but it will come to nothing.' Paul applies the same principle in Acts 13:11 when Elymas the sorcerer opposes the gospel and is struck blind. The hand raised against God's word is the hand that is withered; the hand extended in worship is the hand that is restored (cf. v6).
5: A shadow: the altar was torn apart and the ashes poured out — the sign was fulfilled immediately. The torn altar at Bethel — the counterfeit sacrificial site — anticipates the torn curtain at the Jerusalem temple (Mark 15:38). Both tearings signal that the old sacrificial order is ended: at Bethel, because it was apostate from the beginning; at Jerusalem, because the once-for-all sacrifice has been offered. The ashes poured out signal the end of the sacrifice: 'For by a single offering he has perfected for all time those who are being sanctified' (Hebrews 10:14).
6: A revelation: Jeroboam asked the man of God to entreat YHWH and pray that his hand be restored — and it was. The apostate king appeals to the prophetic mediator; the prophet intercedes; grace is granted. This is the pattern of intercession: Moses interceding for Israel after the golden calf (Exodus 32:11-14), Christ interceding for sinners at the cross (Luke 23:34). The mercy shown to Jeroboam's withered hand — restored without any repentance — is pure grace; but Jeroboam does not receive it as an invitation to repent.
7: A shadow: Jeroboam invited the man of God home to eat — the king's hospitality offered to the prophet after the humbling miracle. The royal invitation is a temptation to compromise: to accept the king's table is to signal accommodation to his regime. Jesus refused similar accommodations: 'A man once gave a great banquet and invited many... I have bought a field / I have bought five yoke of oxen / I have married a wife' (Luke 14:16-20). The prophetic call demands refusal of the world's hospitality when it would compromise the message.
8: A shadow: the man of God refused: 'If you give me half your house, I would not go in with you... for so it was commanded me by the word of the LORD.' The word of YHWH as the absolute authority that overrides royal hospitality. Daniel's refusal to eat from the king's table (Daniel 1:8) follows the same pattern: the servant of YHWH will not be co-opted by the king's provision. Christ refused to be made king by force (John 6:15) and refused the temptations of bread and earthly dominion (Matthew 4:3-10).
9: A shadow: 'You shall not eat bread or drink water or return by the same way that you came.' The three prohibitions — no eating, no drinking, no returning the same way — mark the prophet as one under a unique divine commission whose separateness is the sign of his authority. The NT pattern: 'Do not be conformed to this world' (Romans 12:2); 'Do not love the world or the things in the world' (1 John 2:15). The prophet's separateness is the separateness of holiness: 'I have chosen you out of the world' (John 15:19).
10: A shadow: he went another way and did not return by the way he came to Bethel. The prophet's alternative route is a physical enactment of his spiritual distinctness. The Magi, warned in a dream, 'returned to their country by another route' (Matthew 2:12) — avoiding Herod's trap. In both cases, divine guidance redirects the route to preserve the messenger and the mission.
11: A shadow: there was an old prophet living in Bethel. The presence of a prophet in the very city of apostasy — someone who knows the prophetic office but has accommodated to the corrupt environment — is the figure of the compromised insider. The danger of the Bethel prophet is that he wears prophetic credentials while having accommodated to an apostate system. The NT's 'false prophets who come in sheep's clothing' (Matthew 7:15) wear the same credentials.
12: A shadow: the old prophet's sons told him which way the man of God had gone. The information network of the apostate community tracking the movements of the true prophet: the surveillance of the hostile establishment against the authentic messenger. Jesus's movements were similarly tracked: 'The Pharisees heard that Jesus was making and baptizing more disciples than John' (John 4:1).
13: A shadow: the old prophet said to his sons, 'Saddle the donkey for me.' The donkey as the vehicle of prophetic and royal travel echoes the central donkey of Zechariah 9:9 — the messianic king coming 'riding on a donkey.' Every donkey in the narrative world of the prophets carries this resonance: the king's animal, the prophet's transport, fulfilled finally in the Palm Sunday entry (Matthew 21:7).
14: A shadow: the old prophet found the man of God sitting under an oak. The solitary prophet resting under a tree — vulnerable, at rest — is the condition of the exhausted servant of God. Elijah collapses under a juniper tree (1 Kings 19:4); Jesus sleeps in the boat during the storm (Mark 4:38). The servant of God who has accomplished his mission enters a moment of vulnerability that then becomes the site of the next divine encounter.
15: A shadow: 'Come home with me and eat bread.' The repetition of the invitation — offered now by the false prophet rather than the king — escalates the temptation. The false prophet is more dangerous than the king because the false prophet speaks the language of prophetic fellowship. The NT's warning: 'If anyone comes to you and does not bring this teaching, do not receive him into your house or give him any greeting' (2 John 10). The false prophet offering bread is the seduction that destroys from within.
16: A shadow: the man of God's second refusal — 'I cannot return with you or go in with you, nor will I eat bread or drink water with you in this place.' The double prohibition, stated clearly to the old prophet as it was to the king, shows the man of God's obedience. Up to this point he is faithful. The tragedy is that he maintains the right words while about to yield to the right-sounding lie.
17: A shadow: 'For it was said to me by the word of the LORD, You shall neither eat bread nor drink water there, nor return by the way that you came.' The repeated citation of the divine command is the man of God's ground of standing. 'It is written' is the only defense against deception — the same principle Jesus uses against Satan's temptations (Matthew 4:4, 7, 10). To cite the word of YHWH correctly is to have the ground under one's feet.
18: A shadow: 'I also am a prophet like you, and an angel spoke to me by the word of the LORD, saying, Bring him back with you into your house that he may eat bread and drink water.' The false prophet claims divine authority — 'the word of the LORD' — for a message that contradicts the prior word of the LORD. Galatians 1:8: 'Even if we or an angel from heaven should preach to you a gospel contrary to the one we preached to you, let him be accursed.' The test is not the messenger's credentials but whether his message contradicts the prior revealed word.
19: A shadow: the man of God went back and ate and drank at the old prophet's house. The yielding to false prophecy after correct initial refusal is the tragedy of compromised obedience. The man of God knew the word; he had obeyed it; he yielded to a plausible claim to higher revelation. The NT's warning about 'deceitful spirits and teachings of demons' (1 Timothy 4:1) applies here: the deception comes with prophetic packaging. The man of God's failure is not malice but credulity before a figure in authority.
20: A revelation: while they sat at the table, the word of the LORD came to the old prophet who had brought him back. The divine word comes even through the one who lied — YHWH's word is not bound by the vessel's integrity. This is the startling sovereignty of the divine word: it can use even the instrument of deception to deliver its authentic judgment (cf. Caiaphas prophesying without knowing it, John 11:51). YHWH speaks through Balaam's donkey; he speaks through the compromised prophet.
21: A shadow: 'Because you have been disobedient to the word of the LORD and have not kept the command that the LORD your God commanded you...' The judgment pronounced on the man of God by the same prophet who deceived him: the divine word is now spoken against the man of God through his deceiver. The irony is severe. The NT equivalent: the one who strays from the apostolic word faces the same judgment regardless of his prior faithfulness — 'if we go on sinning deliberately after receiving the knowledge of the truth, there no longer remains a sacrifice for sins' (Hebrews 10:26).
22: A shadow: 'But came back and ate bread and drank water in the place of which he said to you, Eat no bread and drink no water — your body shall not come to the tomb of your fathers.' Death and denial of ancestral burial as the consequence of prophetic disobedience. The extreme penalty underscores that the prophetic office is a weighty calling: 'Not many of you should become teachers, my brothers, for you know that we who teach will be judged with greater strictness' (James 3:1). The servant who knows the master's will and does not do it receives more beatings (Luke 12:47).
23: A shadow: the old prophet saddled the donkey for the man of God's corpse. The solemn preparation for the dead — even a prophet judged by God receives care in death — shows the dignity of the prophetic body. Christ's body was cared for by Joseph of Arimathea and Nicodemus (John 19:38-40); even in death, the prophet's body is not abandoned.
24: A shadow: a lion met him on the road and killed him, and his body lay on the road, with the donkey standing beside it. The lion as the instrument of divine judgment (Revelation 5:5 — the Lion of the tribe of Judah as judge). The donkey, untouched and standing guard, and the lion, not eating the body — both details emphasize the supernatural character of the judgment: this is not a natural predator attack but a divine execution with restraint. The judge punishes precisely; he does not destroy beyond the sentence.
25: A shadow: men passed by and saw the body and the lion — they reported it in the city of the old prophet. The public nature of divine judgment: the body on the road is a warning to all who travel that way. Hebrews 12:11: 'For the moment all discipline seems painful rather than pleasant, but later it yields the peaceful fruit of righteousness to those who have been trained by it.' The public judgment on the disobedient prophet is a training instrument for all who hear of it.
26: A shadow: the old prophet heard and said, 'It is the man of God who was disobedient to the word of the LORD.' The compromised prophet correctly identifies the divine judgment on the one he deceived. The recognition of YHWH's hand in judgment — even by those who contributed to the failure — is the beginning of the fear of God. James 2:13: 'Mercy triumphs over judgment' — but judgment first must be recognized as such.
27: A shadow: the old prophet said to his sons, 'Saddle the donkey for me.' The second saddling: the first was to pursue the man of God with a lie; the second is to honor the man of God with burial. The donkey that carried the deceiver's hospitality now carries the deceiver's repentance. Divine judgment can produce repentance in the one who administered the trap.
28: A shadow: the lion standing beside the body, not eating it, and the donkey also standing — both unharmed, the lion not moving. The supernatural restraint of the predator protecting rather than consuming the dead prophet shows the precision of divine judgment. The lion executed the sentence; the sentence was delivered; the lion is now guardian. The eschatological imagery: Christ as Lion (Revelation 5:5) who judges the wicked but 'will not break a bruised reed or quench a smoldering wick' (Matthew 12:20) with those who belong to him.
29: A shadow: the old prophet laid the man of God's body in his own tomb and mourned over him: 'Alas, my brother!' The mourner is the one who caused the death. This is the profound irony of human complicity in divine judgment — the agent mourns the consequence of his own action. The NT parallel: those who 'look on him whom they have pierced' and 'mourn for him as one mourns for an only child' (Zechariah 12:10; John 19:37) — mourning the one whose death they contributed to.
30: A shadow: the old prophet laid the body in his own tomb and mourned. The gift of burial in the old prophet's tomb — a permanent resting place — is an act of honor for the true prophet even by the one who failed him. Joseph of Arimathea's donation of his own tomb for Jesus (Matthew 27:60) echoes this: the powerful man who honors the prophet's body when the prophet himself is unable to secure burial.
31: A shadow: 'When I die, bury me in the tomb in which the man of God is buried.' The old prophet's final request is to share his burial with the true prophet he deceived — a permanent act of identification with the authentic word he compromised. The principle: the bones that rest beside the true prophet's bones will be protected with him (see 2 Kings 13:21, where a dead man revived when he touched Elisha's bones). The resurrection-hope is in proximity to the true prophet.
32: A fulfillment: 'The saying that he cried by the word of the LORD against the altar in Bethel, and against all the houses of the high places in the cities of Samaria, shall surely come to pass.' The confirmation of the man of God's word — even by the prophet who deceived him — seals the prophecy. The word of the LORD endures: 'The grass withers and the flower falls, but the word of the Lord remains forever' (1 Peter 1:24-25). Even the man of God's disobedience and death do not annul the word he spoke.
33: A shadow: 'After this thing Jeroboam did not turn from his evil way, but made priests for the high places again from among all the people.' The persistent apostasy after divine warning, miraculous signs, and public judgment is the hardening pattern of Pharaoh: 'Pharaoh hardened his heart at this time also, neither would he let the people go' (Exodus 8:32). Romans 1:21-28 describes the same spiral: knowing God, not honoring him, given over to a debased mind. Jeroboam's persistence is the portrait of irreversible apostasy.
34: A shadow: 'This thing became sin to the house of Jeroboam, so as to cut it off and to destroy it from the face of the earth.' The 'sin of Jeroboam' formula that will appear throughout Kings identifies institutional false worship as the defining national sin. The principle is covenant: persistent rejection of the means of grace produces exclusion from the covenant community. The NT equivalent: 'If we go on sinning deliberately after receiving the knowledge of the truth, there no longer remains a sacrifice for sins, but a fearful expectation of judgment' (Hebrews 10:26-27).
